sources_audited.note: The page directly defines NAICS 334413 and lists the cited semiconductor, wafer, optoelectronic, photovoltaic, rectifier, thyristor, and transistor activities.

O*NET directly supports the cited technician tasks, computerized controls, inspection, records, physical handling, maintenance, and hazardous or high-consequence work, but it covers one occupation and supplies neither total-industry task-time shares nor measured AI exposure or realization.

NIST directly supports semiconductor inspection, metrology, defect detection and classification, calibration, process control, and yield relevance, but the article concerns an SEM measurement technique rather than AI deployment, labor savings, or LMM-fab adoption.

GlobalFoundries documents design handoff, tapeout, MPW, qualification, ramp, sustained volume manufacturing, technical engagement, and lifecycle continuity, but it does not measure the eligible-firm share or retained AI-created value for LMM manufacturers.

Gallup directly reports a 2024 cross-industry survey in which 14% of working owners planned to sell, go public, or transfer ownership within five years, but it does not observe semiconductor transactions or financeable acquisition availability.

SIA directly reports 2024 global sales, the WSTS 2025 forecast, AI and communications or vehicle demand drivers, announced U.S. investment, and projected capacity expansion, but not five-year real demand for the LMM specialty subset.
NIST directly supports broad semiconductor end uses and the CHIPS Act funding split for domestic R&D, facilities, and equipment, but it does not quantify five-year LMM specialty demand.

caveats: The a and rho bases remain judgmental: S2 and S3 establish a mixed digital and embodied task environment and the relevance of metrology data, but do not quantify total labor exposure, generative-AI savings, deployment cost, or realization at LMM specialty fabs. This is honestly reflected by PROXY and ESTIMATE labels and a wide H range.

S4 is a large-platform partner-ecosystem example. It supports the existence and mechanics of qualification-to-production services, but not e=0.42 across the frozen firm universe or q=0.56 for LMM manufacturers; the bridges disclose the scale and bargaining-power error directions.

S5's 14% figure combines sale, public offering, and transfer intentions across industries and is not an observed acquisition rate. The s5 bridge and caveats disclose this limitation, and the 0.07-0.22 range appropriately leaves F near the tier boundary.

S6 and S7 support the direction of semiconductor demand and policy investment, not the d5=1.36 five-year real-demand estimate for specialty LMM producers. The aggregate-to-subset extrapolation is disclosed, and lowering demand within the registered range leaves D capped at 10, so the source limitation does not meet the materiality test.

The o=0.98 estimate is supported only directionally by the physical necessity and broad end uses of chips; near-unity survival for every specialty segment is not observed. Its ESTIMATE label and product-obsolescence caveats are honest.

The favorable specialty and repeat-program lens is coherent for an LMM roll-up, but the frozen n=214 anchor is broader and may include fabless, commodity, or capital-starved firms. The packet acknowledges this population-fit risk, and the attainable tier already falls to CONDITIONAL under low research inputs.
summary: Mechanics pass and all seven primitive-cited sources are reachable. The record is useful and candid: direct evidence establishes the industry boundary, fab task mix, metrology functions, qualification-to-production workflow, broad succession intentions, and semiconductor demand or policy direction, while quantitative LMM mappings are explicitly labeled as proxies or estimates with plausible error directions. None of the remaining source or lens limitations independently requires a correction that changes the base tier or the already restricted VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action, so there is no material finding.
